construct_description: The ability to review incoming prescriptions against a patient's medication profile, identify allergy conflicts, drug-drug interactions, duplicate therapy, and dosing concerns — and communicate flagged items clearly to the pharmacist before dispensing.

pba_category.name: Simulation-Based Assessment
pba_category.description: A simulated prescription queue requiring cross-referencing against a patient profile under realistic time pressure.

categories.label: CLEAR
categories.description: No issues identified — proceed to pharmacist final check

categories.label: REVIEW
categories.description: Potential concern — flag for pharmacist verification

categories.label: HOLD
categories.description: Allergy conflict or critical issue — do not fill without resolution

prescriptions.label: Rx #1
prescriptions.rationale: Amoxicillin is a penicillin-class antibiotic. Patient has a documented penicillin allergy (hives, 2019). This is a direct allergy conflict — do not fill without pharmacist and prescriber resolution.
prescriptions.teaching_point: Always cross-check antibiotic class against allergy list — not just the specific drug name. 'Amoxicillin' doesn't appear in the allergy list, but it IS a penicillin. Knowing drug classes is what makes this catch possible.

prescriptions.label: Rx #2
prescriptions.rationale: Multiple concerns: (1) NSAIDs + warfarin = significantly elevated bleeding risk. (2) NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated in CKD stage 3 — can further reduce GFR. (3) NSAIDs can blunt the antihypertensive effect of lisinopril. Needs pharmacist evaluation — may need to recommend acetaminophen instead.
prescriptions.teaching_point: Ibuprofen looks routine but this patient has three reasons it's concerning: warfarin interaction, CKD, and lisinopril. No single flag makes it a HOLD — but the combination demands pharmacist judgment before dispensing.

prescriptions.label: Rx #3
prescriptions.rationale: Patient is already on Lisinopril 10mg daily from a current prescription. This new Rx is for 20mg — possibly an intentional dose increase ordered by cardiology, or possibly a duplicate. The tech cannot determine intent. Pharmacist must verify: intentional titration or error?
prescriptions.teaching_point: Duplicate therapy is a common source of medication errors. When you see the same drug prescribed twice at different doses, it's almost never obviously wrong — it might be a dose increase. That's exactly why it needs a pharmacist review, not a tech judgment call.

prescriptions.label: Rx #4
prescriptions.rationale: Furosemide (loop diuretic) is a new medication with no allergy conflicts, no significant interactions with current medications at this dose, and an appropriate indication for this patient's profile. Proceed to pharmacist final check.
prescriptions.teaching_point: Not every prescription has a problem — and recognizing that is part of the skill. Furosemide at 20mg with this profile is straightforward. Over-flagging wastes pharmacist time and undermines the signal value of your genuine flags.

prescriptions.label: Rx #5
prescriptions.rationale: Two critical issues: (1) Sulfamethoxazole contains a sulfonamide — patient has a documented sulfa drug allergy (rash, 2021). Direct allergy conflict. (2) TMP-SMX significantly potentiates warfarin, raising INR and bleeding risk — a clinically significant interaction even independent of the allergy. Double flag: allergy conflict + serious interaction.
prescriptions.teaching_point: TMP-SMX is one of the most common sources of allergy errors because techs look for 'sulfa' in the allergy list and don't recognize that 'Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole' contains it. Learn the brand names and full chemical names of common sulfa drugs.

pharmacist_note.prompt: Write a brief note to the pharmacist summarizing what you flagged and why. Be specific — include drug names, the issue type, and what you need them to verify or resolve.
pharmacist_note.placeholder: Pharmacist — flagging the following items from Margaret Chen's queue before processing...

partial_rules.reason: Missed allergy conflict severity — dangerous under-flag
Critical miss — allergy conflict not caught

performance_labels.text: Strong screening skills with minor gaps — solid tech instincts 👏
Good foundation — review allergy class recognition and NSAID interactions 📋
Key interactions missed — particularly the drug-class allergy flags ⚠️
Critical catches missed — allergy screening needs focused review 🚨
